docs.title: ZiCharge Merchant Payment API — Developer Documentation
docs.text:
Version: v3 (Java engine) Audience: Merchant developers, integration engineers, partner solution architects Last reviewed: 2026-05-14 Status: Production

Welcome to the ZiCharge Merchant Payment API. The API lets your platform accept payments from ZiCharge wallet holders through a hosted payment page, direct API charge, or QR-code checkout. It is the same gateway your customers already know — re-engineered on a Java 21 / Spring Boot 3.2 backend with stronger validation, idempotent state machines, race-free token lifecycle, signed-callback delivery with automatic retries, and a low-latency hot path designed for high-throughput merchant traffic.

The API is wire-compatible with the previous gateway version so that merchants who are live today require no client changes to keep working. New capabilities are additive and opt-in.

docs.title: What's in this folder
docs.text: # Document What you'll find 1 01-overview.md What the gateway does, supported flows, environments, base URLs, key concepts 2 02-authentication.md Merchant credentials, store password, IP allow-listing, IPN HMAC, security model 3 03-payment-flow.md End-to-end checkout flow, sequence diagrams, state machine, timing & expiry 4 04-api-reference.md Every endpoint: URL, request schema, response schema, errors, examples 5 05-ipn-callbacks.md IPN (Instant Payment Notification) contract, payload, retries, signature verification, idempotency on your side 6 06-error-codes.md Catalogue of code values, when they occur, recommended client handling 7 07-testing-and-go-live.md Sandbox, test data, go-live checklist, rotation, observability you can expect 8 08-migration-notes.md Differences vs. the previous gateway, blocker check, backwards-compat guarantees

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
If you have already integrated with the previous version of the gateway, you can keep using the same endpoint paths and payloads. Read 08-migration-notes.md for the small list of behavioural changes you should be aware of.

For a brand new integration, the shortest path is:

  1. Provision a merchant account and a store password from your ZiCharge merchant dashboard.
  2. Configure an IPN listener URL and (optionally) success / cancel / fail redirect URLs.
  3. Call POST /merchant/generate-payment-token from your server to mint a token.
  4. Redirect the customer to https://<gateway-host>/merchant/payment?token=<token>.
  5. Wait for the IPN callback on your listener (signed, retried), then call POST /merchant/validate-payment or POST /merchant/fetch-payment-status from your server to confirm before fulfilling the order.

That is the entire happy path. Everything else in this folder is here to make sure the unhappy paths are also boring.

docs.title: What the gateway does
docs.text:
The ZiCharge Merchant Payment Gateway lets your platform accept payments from any ZiCharge wallet account. From the customer's perspective, it is a one-page checkout where they enter their mobile number and password (or 4-digit PIN) and confirm. From your platform's perspective, it is a small set of HTTP endpoints plus an asynchronous callback (IPN) delivered to a URL you configure.

The gateway is engineered on a Java 21 / Spring Boot 3.2 backend. Highlights that matter to integrators:

  • Stable, versioned contract — the response envelope (messages, data, code) and field names are guaranteed within a major version.
  • Idempotent token lifecycle — minting a token, paying it, cancelling it, and retrying an IPN are all safe to re-issue without producing duplicate state.
  • Race-free state transitions — the payment record uses pessimistic locking and conditional updates so concurrent pay / cancel / expiry resolve to a single, consistent outcome.
  • At-least-once IPN delivery with automatic retries — IPN delivery is cooldown-gated and replayable; you will receive every terminal event eventually, even if your listener is briefly unreachable.
  • SSRF-hardened callbacks — outbound IPN traffic is restricted by an allow-list policy on the gateway side.
  • Stateless, horizontally scalable JVM tier — under a TLS-terminating load balancer with a Web Application Firewall.

docs.title: Supported flows
docs.text:
Flow What it is Best for Hosted Payment Page Mint a token → redirect customer to the gateway's hosted page → IPN + validation Web checkouts, mobile webviews, QR-code-to-link Direct Charge Mint a token → submit customer credentials to POST /merchant/payment/direct from a trusted channel Custom checkout surfaces under strict PCI-equivalent controls QR Token Same as hosted page, but the response also includes a server-generated QR URL In-store POS, kiosks, printed media Cash-Back Push a refund / promo credit from the merchant wallet to a customer wallet Refunds, loyalty rewards

All four flows share the same authentication and the same IPN contract.

docs.title: Environments
docs.text:
Environment Gateway base URL Purpose Sandbox https://dev.zicharge.com Integration testing, no real funds movement Production https://secure.zicharge.com Live transactions

All traffic is HTTPS-only with TLS 1.2 or higher. HTTP downgrade is not supported.

docs.title: API path conventions
docs.text:
Two equivalent path prefixes are exposed:

  • /api/v3/merchant/... — the canonical, versioned path. Recommended for new integrations.
  • /merchant/... — a flat path retained for backwards compatibility with integrations that started on the previous gateway. No behavioural difference.

Both prefixes serve the same controllers, the same validation, and the same response envelope. Pick one and stick with it per integration.

docs.title: Content types
docs.text:
Endpoint family JSON application/x-www-form-urlencoded multipart/form-data Generate token ✅ ✅ ✅ Validate / status / token-data ✅ ✅ ✅ Direct payment ✅ ✅ ✅ Cash-back ✅ ✅ ✅ Transactions list ✅ (GET) n/a n/a IPN delivered to your listener n/a ✅ (we POST form-urlencoded to you) n/a

JSON is preferred for new integrations. Form-encoded variants exist so that integrations that were originally form-based keep working without changes.

docs.title: Response envelope
docs.text:
Every endpoint returns HTTP 200, including when the outcome is a business-level failure. The real outcome is carried inside the JSON body in the code field:

{
  "code": 200,
  "messages": ["Successfully Deposited."],
  "data": { ... }
}
Field Type Meaning code integer The logical outcome. 200 = success. Non-success values are documented in 06-error-codes.md. messages array of strings One or more localized human-readable messages. Always an array. data object | array | null Endpoint-specific payload. null on errors.

Why HTTP 200 instead of HTTP 4xx? This contract is identical to the previous gateway and is preserved on purpose. Some legacy proxies and merchant SDKs treat non-2xx HTTP statuses as transport failures and retry, which can cause duplicate side-effects. Keeping the logical outcome in the body lets your client decide retry semantics safely.

Endpoints that pre-date the v3 prefix (POST /merchant/generate-payment-token, POST /merchant/payment/direct) use an extended legacy envelope that adds top-level fields (token, transactionInfo) alongside messages and code. The exact shape is documented per-endpoint in 04-api-reference.md.

docs.title: Amounts and currency
docs.text:
• All amounts are in IQD (Iraqi Dinar).
  • Amounts are transmitted as decimal numbers; integer amounts are required for transaction limits and are recommended for bill_amount.
  • The minimum bill_amount is 250 IQD at token generation. Sub-minimum tokens are rejected before they are created.
  • Amounts are validated with BigDecimal precision on the server. There is no floating-point rounding in the payment path.

docs.title: Locale & internationalization
docs.text:
• The gateway honours the Accept-Language request header.
  • Supported languages: English (en), Arabic (ar), Kurdish (ku).
  • Some endpoints also accept a lang field in the body for explicit override; when both are provided, the body field wins.
  • Error messages, success messages, and the hosted payment page are all localized.

docs.title: Identifiers used in this API
docs.text: Identifier Format Source Use merchant_mobile_no +964 + 10 digits, optional leading 0 inside the local part Provisioned at merchant onboarding Identifies the merchant wallet on every server-to-server call store_password Opaque string Provisioned at merchant onboarding Authenticates server-to-server calls; never exposed to customers order_id Opaque string, must be unique per merchant Your system Idempotency anchor — same order_id on token mint won't double-charge payment_token Opaque token, ≥ 32 chars Returned by generate-payment-token Bound to one order_id and one validity window transaction_id Numeric, server-issued Returned in IPN and validation responses Use this in your books as the canonical ZiCharge reference customer_mobile_no +964 + 10 digits Set by the customer at checkout, optionally pre-filled The paying account

docs.title: Token lifecycle (at a glance)
docs.text:
created           paid           validated
  ─────────►───────────────►────────────────►
                 ▲                ▲
       cancelled │                │ fetch-status / validate-payment
       (user / system / timeout)  │
                 │                │
                 └─► IPN dispatched (Success | Cancelled)

A token is valid for 15 minutes from creation. After 15 minutes it can no longer be paid. If a customer opens an already-expired token, the gateway will mark it cancelled and fire a Cancelled IPN so your system can release the cart.

docs.title: Where to go next
docs.text:
• New integration → 03-payment-flow.md then 04-api-reference.md.
  • Existing integration → 08-migration-notes.md first.
  • Building the IPN listener → 05-ipn-callbacks.md.
  • Going to production → 07-testing-and-go-live.md.

docs.title: 2. Authentication & Security
docs.text:
The gateway exposes three distinct trust boundaries. Make sure you understand which one each call uses before you ship.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 1. Server-to-Server (merchant backend → ZiCharge)                  │
│    merchant_mobile_no + store_password                             │
│    HTTPS + (optional) IP allow-list                                │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 2. Customer-facing (customer browser → ZiCharge hosted page)       │
│    Bearer token (`payment_token`) in URL query                     │
│    Customer authenticates with their own wallet mobile + password  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 3. Asynchronous (ZiCharge → merchant IPN listener)                 │
│    Form-POST to merchant-configured ipn_url                        │
│    Recommended: HMAC verification on receipt (see §2.4)            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 2.1 Merchant credentials
docs.text:
Two values, both provisioned at merchant onboarding through the ZiCharge merchant dashboard:

Credential Purpose merchant_mobile_no Public identifier of your merchant wallet. Format +964XXXXXXXXXX. store_password Shared secret bound to your store. Never share it with customers and never embed it in client-side code or mobile binaries.

These values are sent in the request body of server-to-server calls. They are validated as a paired credential — the gateway will not tell you whether the failure was the mobile or the password (this prevents account enumeration).

docs.title: 2.2 Where these credentials are accepted
docs.text: Endpoint merchant_mobile_no store_password POST /merchant/generate-payment-token required required POST /merchant/generate-qr-token required required POST /merchant/validate-payment required required POST /merchant/fetch-payment-status required n/a (order ID + amount serve as proof of knowledge) POST /merchant/fetch-payment-token-data n/a n/a (read-only, token is the lookup key) POST /merchant/cash-back required required POST /merchant/payment/direct n/a (customer credentials, not merchant) n/a

docs.title: 2.3 Network controls
docs.text:
Two controls are available beyond the credential pair. Both are configured from the merchant dashboard and are strongly recommended for production:

  1. Source IP allow-list — the gateway will reject server-to-server calls that arrive from any IP outside the list you provision. Use this if your platform issues outbound calls from a stable egress range.
  2. TLS enforcement — HTTPS is the only accepted scheme. There is no HTTP listener to fall back to.

docs.title: 2.4 IPN (callback) authenticity
docs.text:
The gateway delivers payment-outcome callbacks to the ipn_url you configure. The default callback is a application/x-www-form-urlencoded POST. To prove the callback originated from ZiCharge, two layers are available:

Layer 1 — connection-level. Configure your firewall to accept POSTs to your IPN listener only from the ZiCharge production egress range published in the merchant dashboard. This is the simplest defence and is sufficient for most merchants.

Layer 2 — payload signature (recommended for new integrations). When enabled on your merchant configuration, every IPN includes:

  • X-ZiCharge-Signature HTTP header — hex-encoded HMAC-SHA256 of the raw form body, signed with your ipn_secret.
  • X-ZiCharge-Timestamp HTTP header — Unix epoch seconds at dispatch time.
  • X-ZiCharge-Request-Id HTTP header — opaque request ID, useful for correlating with your logs and the gateway's logs.

To verify:

import hmac, hashlib
expected = hmac.new(
    ipn_secret.encode("utf-8"),
    raw_body_bytes,
    hashlib.sha256
).hexdigest()
if not hmac.compare_digest(expected, received_signature):
    return reject()
# Also enforce: abs(now - X-ZiCharge-Timestamp) < 300 seconds

Implementation notes:

  • Compare signatures in constant time (hmac.compare_digest, MessageDigest.isEqual) to prevent timing attacks.
  • Reject any IPN where the timestamp drifts more than ±5 minutes from your server clock.
  • Rotate the ipn_secret from the merchant dashboard; the gateway supports two active secrets during rotation windows.

If your merchant configuration does not have payload signatures enabled yet, contact integrations support to switch it on. Existing integrations continue to work with Layer 1 only.

docs.title: 2.5 Idempotency
docs.text:
The gateway is idempotent on the inputs that matter:

  • Token mint — calling generate-payment-token repeatedly with the same merchant_mobile_no + order_id while a token is still valid will return the existing token; it will not create a duplicate.
  • Direct payment — protected by the token's lifecycle state machine. The same payment_token cannot be charged twice; the second attempt fails with a token_already_used error.
  • IPN delivery — every IPN we send carries an X-ZiCharge-Request-Id header. We may re-deliver after a transient error; deduplicate on your side using this ID plus order_id.

docs.title: 2.6 Replay protection
docs.text: For server-to-server calls, idempotency keys plus the short token validity window (15 minutes) bound replay risk. For IPN traffic, the X-ZiCharge-Timestamp + signature pair provides explicit replay protection.

docs.title: 2.7 Sensitive data handling
docs.text:
• Never log store_password, payment_token, or customer credentials.
  • Never include store_password or payment_token in URLs (use POST body).
  • Never expose your store_password to a customer browser; all credentialed calls are server-to-server only.
  • The gateway redacts sensitive fields in its own logs as a defence in depth; that is not a substitute for your own discipline.

docs.title: 2.8 Internal endpoint protection
docs.text: A small set of /internal/merchant/* endpoints exist for scheduler use (cancel-IPN retry, reconciliation). These are not part of the public surface, are protected by a separate X-Internal-Auth shared secret compared in constant time, and are additionally restricted at the load balancer / ingress layer. They are documented here only so that you do not waste time discovering them in network traces.

docs.title: 3. Payment Flow
docs.text: This chapter walks through the end-to-end checkout from the moment your customer presses "Pay" until your order is fulfilled. Read this before implementing — the API reference will then read as a glossary rather than a puzzle.

docs.title: 3.1 Sequence — happy path (hosted page)
docs.text:
Customer        Your Backend                ZiCharge Gateway              Your IPN Listener
   │                │                              │                                │
   │  add to cart   │                              │                                │
   │ ──────────────►│                              │                                │
   │                │                              │                                │
   │                │ POST /merchant/generate-     │                                │
   │                │      payment-token           │                                │
   │                │ ────────────────────────────►│                                │
   │                │                              │  mint token (15-min validity)  │
   │                │◄──────────────────────────── │  { code:200, token:"..." }     │
   │                │                              │                                │
   │  HTTP 302 to   │                              │                                │
   │  ZiCharge      │                              │                                │
   │◄───────────────│                              │                                │
   │ GET /merchant/payment?token=...               │                                │
   │ ─────────────────────────────────────────────►│                                │
   │  hosted page (mobile + password / PIN)        │                                │
   │◄───────────────────────────────────────────── │                                │
   │                │                              │                                │
   │  submit credentials                           │                                │
   │ ─────────────────────────────────────────────►│  authenticate, debit, credit   │
   │                │                              │  mark token USED               │
   │  success screen + auto-redirect to            │                                │
   │  merchant success_url                         │                                │
   │◄───────────────────────────────────────────── │                                │
   │                │                              │                                │
   │                │                              │  AFTER_COMMIT, async:          │
   │                │                              │  POST ipn_url (form-encoded)   │
   │                │                              │ ──────────────────────────────►│
   │                │                              │                                │  verify signature,
   │                │                              │                                │  store transaction,
   │                │                              │                                │  HTTP 200 OK
   │                │                              │◄────────────────────────────── │
   │                │                              │                                │
   │                │  POST /merchant/             │                                │
   │                │       fetch-payment-status   │                                │
   │                │ ────────────────────────────►│                                │
   │                │  { code:200, paid:true,      │                                │
   │                │    message:"Successfully     │                                │
   │                │    Deposited." }             │                                │
   │                │◄──────────────────────────── │                                │
   │                │                              │                                │
   │                │  fulfil order                │                                │

Two confirmation channels — the IPN and the synchronous fetch-payment-status / validate-payment — are deliberate. Either one is authoritative on its own; both together are how a robust integration confirms before fulfilling.

docs.title: 3.2 Sequence — customer cancels or times out
docs.text:
Customer        Your Backend                ZiCharge Gateway              Your IPN Listener
   │                │                              │                                │
   │  on hosted page, clicks Cancel ✕              │                                │
   │  (or 15-min timer hits 0)                     │                                │
   │ ─────────────────────────────────────────────►│  mark token CANCELLED          │
   │                │                              │  (first-write-wins lock)       │
   │  "Payment cancelled" → redirect to            │                                │
   │  merchant cancel_url                          │                                │
   │◄───────────────────────────────────────────── │                                │
   │                │                              │                                │
   │                │                              │  AFTER_COMMIT, async:          │
   │                │                              │  POST ipn_url                  │
   │                │                              │   status=Cancelled             │
   │                │                              │ ──────────────────────────────►│
   │                │                              │  retry policy (see §3.4)       │

docs.title: 3.3 State machine — payment token
docs.text:
┌───────────┐
                  │  PENDING  │  token minted, not paid, not cancelled, within validity window
                  └─────┬─────┘
                        │
        ┌───────────────┼────────────────────┬────────────────────┐
        │               │                    │                    │
        ▼               ▼                    ▼                    ▼
   Customer pays   Customer cancels    Timer expires while   Customer opens an
                   on hosted page      page is open          already-expired link
        │               │                    │                    │
        ▼               ▼                    ▼                    ▼
   ┌────────┐     ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐
   │  USED  │     │CANCELLED │         │CANCELLED │         │CANCELLED │
   │ (paid) │     │ by USER  │         │ by USER  │         │by SYSTEM │
   └────────┘     │USER_CAN- │         │TIMEOUT_  │         │TIMEOUT_  │
                  │ CELLED   │         │CLIENT    │         │DIRECT_   │
                  └──────────┘         └──────────┘         │OPEN      │
                                                            └──────────┘
                       │                    │                    │
                       └────────────────────┴────────────────────┘
                                            │
                                IPN dispatched (Success | Cancelled)

Transitions are guarded by:

  • A pessimistic WRITE lock on the token row during direct-payment processing (3-second wait timeout — fail-fast under contention).
  • A conditional UPDATE on cancel (WHERE cancelled_at IS NULL) — first-write-wins, no silent overwrites.
  • Idempotent dispatch claim before each IPN send — duplicate IPNs are physically impossible across overlapping retry runs.

docs.title: 3.4 IPN delivery & retries
docs.text:
When a terminal state is reached, the gateway publishes a domain event. After the database transaction commits, an asynchronous listener POSTs the IPN to your ipn_url.

Delivery semantics:

  • At-least-once. You may receive the same IPN more than once; deduplicate using order_id + X-ZiCharge-Request-Id.
  • Order: for a single token, you will never receive a Cancelled IPN after a Success IPN. The state machine prevents both terminal states.
  • Timing: IPNs are dispatched on the gateway's asynchronous worker pool, typically within a few hundred milliseconds of commit. Treat anything under 5 seconds as nominal.

Retry policy for failed deliveries (your listener was unreachable, returned non-2xx, or timed out):

Attempt When 1 Immediately after commit 2..N Driven by an internal scheduler with a per-token cooldown (default several minutes) Max 8 attempts per terminal event

Between attempts the gateway records:

  • ipn_cancel_attempts (or success-side equivalent) — counter, monotonic.
  • ipn_cancel_last_attempt_at — last dispatch timestamp; used by the cooldown gate to prevent duplicate retries across overlapping scheduler runs.
  • ipn_cancel_fired_at — set only after a 2xx confirmation; absence indicates "not yet successfully delivered".
  • The last error string (class + truncated message, max 500 chars) for support diagnosis.

After 8 failed attempts the token stops being picked up. Your reconciliation flow should use POST /merchant/fetch-payment-status on order timeout to recover.

What your listener must do for retries to behave well:

  • Respond with HTTP 2xx only after you have persisted the IPN. A 2xx response is taken as proof of delivery — we will not retry.
  • If you cannot persist yet (database unavailable), respond with HTTP 5xx. We will retry.
  • Time-limit your own processing — slow listeners (> 10 seconds) may be considered failed and retried.

docs.title: 3.5 Direct payment flow
docs.text:
For merchants integrating their own customer-facing checkout (mobile app, in-store POS, custom web flow), the direct payment endpoint accepts the customer's credentials and the payment_token in a single server-to-server call:

POST /merchant/payment/direct
{
  "mobile_no":     "+9647701234567",
  "password":      "<customer-password>",
  "payment_token": "<token-from-step-1>"
}

The flow is otherwise identical — token transitions to USED, IPN is fired, the success envelope is returned synchronously.

Direct payment requires that your channel be subject to PCI-equivalent controls on handling customer passwords. Specifically:

  • Never persist the customer's password.
  • Use a dedicated transport that you control end-to-end.
  • Prefer the hosted page for any web-browser context.

docs.title: 3.6 Reconciliation
docs.text:
Reconciliation has two server-to-server endpoints. Use them when the IPN is late, lost, or your listener was being deployed:

Endpoint Use it when POST /merchant/fetch-payment-status You know the order_id and the amount — fast, exact match POST /merchant/validate-payment You want the full transaction detail (timestamp, customer account, transaction id)

Both endpoints are read-only and safe to call as often as needed.

docs.title: 3.7 Timing — what to expect
docs.text:
Operation Typical P99 budget generate-payment-token < 100 ms 300 ms validate-payment / fetch-payment-status < 80 ms 250 ms payment/direct end-to-end (synchronous portion) < 500 ms 1.5 s Token validity window n/a 15 minutes from mint IPN dispatch after commit < 1 s nominal retries up to 8 over a multi-minute window

These are gateway-side budgets and exclude your own backend latency. Configure your HTTP clients with explicit connect / read timeouts (suggest 2 s connect, 10 s read).

docs.title: 4. API Reference
docs.text:
All endpoints return HTTP 200; the logical outcome is in the body code field. Paths shown without prefix are mounted under both /api/v3/merchant and /merchant.

Base URLs:

  • Sandbox — https://dev.zicharge.com
  • Production — https://secure.zicharge.com

docs.title: POST /merchant/generate-payment-token
docs.text: Mint a one-time payment token for a checkout. Idempotent per (merchant_mobile_no, order_id) while a prior token is still valid.

docs.title: Request — JSON
docs.text:
POST /merchant/generate-payment-token HTTP/1.1
Host: secure.zicharge.com
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "merchant_mobile_no": "+9647712345678",
  "store_password":     "your-store-secret",
  "order_id":           "ORD-2026-000123",
  "bill_amount":        5000,
  "success_url":        "https://shop.example.com/orders/123/success",
  "cancel_url":         "https://shop.example.com/orders/123/cancel",
  "fail_url":           "https://shop.example.com/orders/123/fail",
  "customer_mobile_no": "+9647701234567"
}

docs.title: Request — form-urlencoded
docs.text: The same fields as the JSON body are accepted as form fields. Use this only for parity with older integrations.

docs.title: Field reference
docs.text: Field Type Required Notes merchant_mobile_no string yes +964XXXXXXXXXX, validated by Iraqi mobile-number regex store_password string yes Shared secret from the merchant dashboard order_id string yes Your unique order identifier; idempotency anchor bill_amount number yes IQD; minimum 250; whole numbers recommended success_url URL no Customer redirected here after payment; falls back to merchant default cancel_url URL no Customer redirected here on cancel; falls back to merchant default fail_url URL no Customer redirected here on failure; falls back to merchant default customer_mobile_no string no Pre-fill the customer's mobile on the hosted page; format +964XXXXXXXXXX if present

docs.title: Success response (legacy envelope, preserved for client compatibility)
docs.text:
{
  "messages": [],
  "token":    "tkn_4f8a6e1fa8df48e88ef49a8sd7f458ve4s4dfgpkk",
  "code":     200
}

Redirect your customer to:

https://secure.zicharge.com/merchant/payment?token=<token>

docs.title: Common error responses
docs.text:
code When 422 Validation failure — missing field, malformed mobile, sub-minimum amount; messages contains one entry per failed field 401 Invalid merchant_mobile_no / store_password pair 409 Order already paid by a previous token 500 Unexpected — log the X-ZiCharge-Request-Id response header and contact support

ℹ️ The canonical, non-legacy path POST /merchant/generate-qr-token returns the same token plus a server-generated qr_url and expires_in_minutes. See below.

docs.title: POST /merchant/generate-qr-token
docs.text: Mints a token and additionally returns a QR-code URL pre-encoded with the payment link. Use this for in-store POS terminals, kiosks, and printed media. The token semantics are otherwise identical to generate-payment-token.

docs.title: Request
docs.text: Same fields as generate-payment-token.

docs.title: Success response
docs.text:
{
  "code": 200,
  "messages": ["ok"],
  "data": {
    "token":              "tkn_4f8a6e1fa8df48e88ef49a8sd7f458ve4s4dfgpkk",
    "qr_url":             "https://secure.zicharge.com/qr/tkn_4f8a6e1fa8df48e88ef49a8sd7f458ve4s4dfgpkk.png",
    "expires_in_minutes": 15
  }
}

docs.title: POST /merchant/validate-payment
docs.text:
Authoritative server-to-server lookup for the result of a completed order. Use this on the reconciliation path or as a synchronous confirm before fulfilling.

Aliases: the path POST /merchant/payment/validation is identical and is preserved for compatibility.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "merchant_mobile_no": "+9647712345678",
  "store_password":     "your-store-secret",
  "order_id":           "ORD-2026-000123"
}

docs.title: Success response
docs.text:
{
  "code": 200,
  "messages": ["Please find your transaction details:"],
  "data": {
    "transaction_id":     123456,
    "order_id":           "ORD-2026-000123",
    "bill_amount":        "5000",
    "customer_account_no":"+9647701234567",
    "status":             "Success",
    "received_at":        "2026-05-14 12:55:45"
  }
}

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: code When 422 Validation failure (missing / malformed field) 422 Credential pair invalid OR order not found — single error to prevent enumeration. messages contains "Sorry! Invalid Store Credentials." or "Sorry! Invalid Order ID."

docs.title: POST /merchant/fetch-payment-status
docs.text:
Fast yes/no check for a specific (order, amount) pair. Cheaper than validate-payment because the lookup is an exact-match on the indexed columns.

Also available as GET /merchant/fetch-payment-status with the same parameters in the query string. The GET form is convenient for retries from systems that prefer idempotent verbs.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "merchant_mobile_no": "+9647712345678",
  "order_id":           "ORD-2026-000123",
  "amount":             5000
}

docs.title: Success response
docs.text:
{
  "code": 200,
  "messages": ["Congratulation! Successfully Deposited."],
  "data": {
    "paid":    true,
    "message": "Congratulation! Successfully Deposited."
  }
}

docs.title: Non-success response (order not yet paid)
docs.text:
{
  "code": 404,
  "messages": ["Awaiting payment for order id #ORD-2026-000123"],
  "data": {
    "paid":    false,
    "message": "Awaiting payment for order id #ORD-2026-000123"
  }
}

HTTP status remains 200; only the body code differs. The exact match is performed at the database level on (merchant_id, order_id, amount) for deterministic behaviour.

docs.title: POST /merchant/fetch-payment-token-data
docs.text:
Returns the public-safe metadata for a token, by token. Used by the hosted payment page; also available to merchants who want to inspect a token's state without performing a validate-payment lookup.

Also available as GET /merchant/fetch-payment-token-data?payment_token=... with CORS enabled for browser-side inspection.

docs.title: Request
docs.text: { "payment_token": "tkn_..." }

docs.title: Success response
docs.text:
{
  "code": 200,
  "messages": ["ok"],
  "data": {
    "token":              "tkn_4f8a6e1fa8df48e88ef49a8sd7f458ve4s4dfgpkk",
    "merchant_mobile_no": "+9647712345678",
    "merchant_name":      "Example Store",
    "order_id":           "ORD-2026-000123",
    "bill_amount":        "5000",
    "success_url":        "https://shop.example.com/orders/123/success",
    "cancel_url":         "https://shop.example.com/orders/123/cancel",
    "fail_url":           "https://shop.example.com/orders/123/fail",
    "customer_mobile_no": "+9647701234567",
    "status":             "PENDING",
    "created_at":         "2026-05-14T12:50:00"
  }
}

status is one of PENDING, USED, CANCELLED.

docs.title: POST /merchant/payment/direct
docs.text: Charge a payment token directly using customer credentials. Server-to-server call from a trusted channel only.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "mobile_no":     "+9647701234567",
  "password":      "<customer-password>",
  "payment_token": "tkn_4f8a6e1fa8df48e88ef49a8sd7f458ve4s4dfgpkk"
}

docs.title: Success response (legacy envelope)
docs.text:
{
  "messages": ["Payment processed successfully."],
  "transactionInfo": {
    "id":                  123456,
    "order_id":            "ORD-2026-000123",
    "bill_amount":         5000,
    "customer_account_no": "+9647701234567",
    "status":              "Success",
    "received_at":         "2026-05-14 12:55:45"
  },
  "code": 200
}

The IPN to your ipn_url is fired asynchronously after commit, identical to the hosted-page flow.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: code When 422 Validation failure 422 "Mobile No. & Password combination do not match." — unified message; intentionally indistinguishable from "blocked account" to prevent enumeration 404 Token unknown 410 Token expired 409 Token already used 409 Token cancelled 422 Insufficient balance, limit exceeded, KYC tier insufficient — exact message in messages[0]

docs.title: GET /merchant/transactions
docs.text: Paginated list of completed transactions for a merchant. Read-only.

docs.title: Query parameters
docs.text: Name Type Default Notes merchantMobileNo string required Your merchant mobile number page int 1 1-indexed page number perPage int 20 Cap 100

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "code": 200,
  "messages": ["ok"],
  "data": {
    "transactions": [
      {
        "transaction_id":     123456,
        "order_id":           "ORD-2026-000123",
        "bill_amount":        "5000",
        "customer_account_no":"+9647701234567",
        "status":             "Success",
        "received_at":        "2026-05-14 12:55:45"
      }
    ],
    "current_page": 1,
    "per_page":     20,
    "total":        4231,
    "message":      "ok"
  }
}

docs.title: POST /merchant/cash-back
docs.text: Push a refund or promotional credit from your merchant wallet to a customer wallet. Settled synchronously.

docs.title: Request
docs.text:
{
  "merchant_mobile_no": "+9647712345678",
  "store_password":     "your-store-secret",
  "receiver_mobile_no": "+9647701234567",
  "amount":             1500,
  "reference_id":       "RFD-2026-000789",
  "lang":               "en"
}

docs.title: Field reference
docs.text: Field Type Required Notes merchant_mobile_no string yes Your merchant wallet store_password string yes receiver_mobile_no string yes Must be an existing customer wallet amount number yes IQD, minimum 250 reference_id string no Your reference; idempotency anchor lang string no en, ar, ku — overrides Accept-Language

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
{
  "code": 200,
  "messages": ["Cash-back processed successfully."],
  "data": {
    "transaction_id":      234567,
    "reference_id":        "RFD-2026-000789",
    "amount":              1500,
    "customer_account_no": "+9647701234567",
    "status":              "Success",
    "sent_at":             "2026-05-14 13:02:11"
  }
}

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: Standard envelope. Notable cases: insufficient merchant balance (code 422), receiver not found (code 422 generic), daily cap exceeded (code 422).

docs.title: GET /merchant/payment?token=<token>
docs.text:
Returns the HTML payment page for the customer. Not an API endpoint per se — redirect your customer here. The page is mobile-responsive, supports iframe embedding via ?iframe=1, and localizes itself based on Accept-Language.

If the token is missing, expired, or already settled, the page renders an explanatory message and (for expired tokens) triggers a server-side cancel that will dispatch a Cancelled IPN.

docs.title: Common request headers
docs.text: Header Notes Content-Type application/json (recommended) or application/x-www-form-urlencoded Accept-Language en, ar, ku — affects message localization X-Request-Id Optional client-supplied request ID; if absent, the gateway assigns one and returns it in X-ZiCharge-Request-Id

docs.title: Common response headers
docs.text: Header Notes X-ZiCharge-Request-Id Echoed from X-Request-Id or freshly assigned. Include this in support requests. Content-Type Always application/json; charset=utf-8 on API endpoints

docs.title: 5. IPN (Instant Payment Notification) Callbacks
docs.text: IPN is the gateway's asynchronous notification to your platform when a payment reaches a terminal state (Success or Cancelled). It is the same mechanism your existing integration already uses; this chapter documents the contract, the retry behaviour, and the verification steps in full.

docs.title: 5.1 Contract
docs.text:
The gateway POSTs to the ipn_url configured for your merchant account.

  • Method: POST
  • Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
  • Body encoding: standard form encoding, UTF-8

docs.title: Payload fields
docs.text: Field Type Success value Cancelled value transaction_id string numeric ledger transaction id empty string ("") order_id string the order_id you submitted at token mint same bill_amount string decimal, e.g. "5000" decimal, e.g. "5000" customer_account_no string customer's mobile, e.g. "+9647701234567" customer's mobile if known, else empty status string "Success" "Cancelled" received_at string "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss" (gateway clock, UTC) same ipn string "1" "1"

docs.title: Headers (when signature verification is enabled on your config)
docs.text: Header Description X-ZiCharge-Signature Hex HMAC-SHA256 of the raw form body, signed with your ipn_secret X-ZiCharge-Timestamp Unix epoch seconds at dispatch time X-ZiCharge-Request-Id Opaque request ID; use to deduplicate and to correlate with support

docs.title: 5.2 Verification — recommended listener pseudocode
docs.text:
def handle_ipn(request):
    body = request.raw_body  # bytes — verify against the raw body, not parsed form

    # 1) Signature
    expected = hmac.new(IPN_SECRET, body, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    signature = request.headers["X-ZiCharge-Signature"]
    if not hmac.compare_digest(expected, signature):
        return Response(status=400)

    # 2) Timestamp drift (±5 minutes)
    ts = int(request.headers["X-ZiCharge-Timestamp"])
    if abs(time.time() - ts) > 300:
        return Response(status=400)

    # 3) Idempotency
    request_id = request.headers["X-ZiCharge-Request-Id"]
    if already_processed(request_id):
        return Response(status=200)   # we already stored it — acknowledge

    # 4) Parse & persist
    fields = parse_form(body)
    assert fields["status"] in ("Success", "Cancelled")
    persist_ipn(fields, request_id)

    # 5) Acknowledge ONLY after persistence
    return Response(status=200)

Three pieces of discipline matter:

  1. Sign-verify against the raw body, not the parsed form. A re-encoded form is not byte-identical and the HMAC will not match.
  2. Constant-time signature comparison (hmac.compare_digest, MessageDigest.isEqual) to avoid timing leakage.
  3. Acknowledge only after persistence. A 2xx response stops retries. If you cannot persist, return 5xx and we will retry.

docs.title: 5.3 Delivery semantics
docs.text:
• At-least-once delivery. Build your listener to be idempotent on X-ZiCharge-Request-Id (preferred) or on order_id + status.
  • No Success after Cancelled and no Cancelled after Success. The token state machine guarantees one terminal outcome per token.
  • Cooldown-gated retries. Concurrent retry workers cannot dispatch the same IPN twice; this is enforced by an atomic claim before each send.
  • Maximum 8 attempts per terminal event. After that, recovery shifts to your synchronous reconciliation calls (fetch-payment-status / validate-payment).

docs.title: 5.4 What "delivery succeeded" means
docs.text:
The gateway records a successful delivery when your listener returns any HTTP 2xx response within the read timeout. The response body is discarded; only the status code matters.

  • HTTP 2xx → delivered, no more retries for this event.
  • HTTP 3xx → not followed; treated as failed; retry scheduled.
  • HTTP 4xx → treated as failed; retry scheduled. Do not respond 4xx unless you genuinely want the gateway to retry — typically this only delays your own processing.
  • HTTP 5xx → failed; retry scheduled.
  • Connection refused / read timeout (~10 s) → failed; retry scheduled.

docs.title: 5.5 SSRF protection on the gateway side
docs.text: The gateway will refuse to POST your IPN to a URL that resolves to an internal-only address range. Configure your ipn_url to a publicly resolvable HTTPS endpoint. URLs that resolve to RFC 1918 ranges, link-local, loopback, or other reserved blocks are rejected and the IPN is dropped (with a structured log on the gateway side).

docs.title: 5.6 Configuring your IPN URL
docs.text:
Set the ipn_url on your merchant configuration through the dashboard. Recommended:

  • HTTPS only.
  • A dedicated path (e.g., /internal/zicharge/ipn) protected by your own ingress.
  • Optional: rotate the ipn_secret from the dashboard. The gateway accepts two active secrets during rotation windows so you can switch over without downtime.

docs.title: 5.7 Observability
docs.text: For every IPN you receive, the gateway has emitted structured events with the same X-ZiCharge-Request-Id. If a delivery is in dispute, supply that header value to support and we can show you the dispatch attempts, response codes, retry timings, and (if signatures were enabled) the bytes signed.

docs.title: 5.8 Synchronous fallback
docs.text:
If your listener has been down longer than the retry window allows, recover with:

  • POST /merchant/fetch-payment-status — fast, returns the boolean outcome.
  • POST /merchant/validate-payment — full detail.

Both endpoints are safe to call repeatedly.

docs.title: 6. Error Codes
docs.text: Every API endpoint returns HTTP 200; the logical outcome is in the body code field. The catalogue below covers every value you should expect to see in production, and the recommended client handling for each.

docs.title: 6.1 Success
docs.text: code Meaning Recommended action 200 Request fulfilled. data contains the endpoint payload. Proceed.

docs.title: 6.2 Client errors
docs.text: code Meaning Where it appears Recommended action 400 Malformed request body / unsupported content type All endpoints Fix the request; do not retry as-is. 401 Authentication required or invalid (merchant credentials) All credentialed endpoints Verify merchant_mobile_no + store_password; do not auto-retry — exponential lockout protects accounts. 404 Resource not found in this context fetch-payment-status, fetch-payment-token-data Treat as "not yet paid / unknown token". For status checks, retry per your reconciliation policy. 409 Conflict — state machine refused the transition Token mint (duplicate order), direct-payment (token already used / cancelled) Do not retry. Inspect with fetch-payment-token-data if needed. 410 Gone — token expired Direct payment, hosted page Mint a new token. 422 Validation failure or business-rule violation All endpoints Read messages[] — each entry corresponds to one rule violation. Fix and retry. 429 Rate-limited Any credentialed endpoint under sustained abuse Back off exponentially. Contact integrations support if you legitimately exceed rate limits.

docs.title: 6.3 Server errors
docs.text: code Meaning Recommended action 500 Unexpected gateway error Retry idempotent reads (fetch-payment-status, validate-payment) with backoff. For writes (generate-payment-token, payment/direct), verify with fetch-payment-status / fetch-payment-token-data before retrying. Include X-ZiCharge-Request-Id in support tickets. 502 / 503 / 504 Transient infrastructure failure (rare; expected only during deploys) Retry with exponential backoff (e.g., 1 s, 2 s, 4 s, up to 30 s total).

docs.title: 6.4 Common messages strings
docs.text:
Localized; the strings below are the canonical English forms.

String Trigger "Sorry! Invalid Store Credentials." Merchant credential pair did not match. Intentionally generic to prevent enumeration. "Sorry! Invalid Order ID." Credentials valid, but the order was not found. "Mobile No. & Password combination do not match." Direct-payment customer credentials invalid or account blocked. Intentionally generic. "Congratulation! Successfully Deposited." Successful payment confirmed by fetch-payment-status. "Awaiting payment for order id #<order_id>" fetch-payment-status did not find a completed match. "The merchant mobile no format is invalid." Phone-number regex failure on merchant_mobile_no. "The customer mobile no format is invalid." Phone-number regex failure on customer_mobile_no. "Bill amount must be at least 250." Sub-minimum amount at token generation. "This payment link has expired." Hosted page accessed after the 15-minute validity window. "Insufficient balance." Direct payment with under-funded customer wallet. "Daily limit exceeded." / "Monthly limit exceeded." Customer or merchant transaction cap reached. "Unable to process service!" Generic fallback for unexpected server errors.

docs.title: 6.5 Validation envelope
docs.text:
When a request fails Bean Validation (@NotBlank, regex, @NotNull, etc.), the response contains one entry per failed field:

{
  "code": 422,
  "messages": [
    "The merchant mobile no format is invalid.",
    "The order id must not be blank."
  ],
  "data": null
}

The messages preserve the same wording your previous integration would have seen, so any client-side string matching you may have stays valid.

docs.title: 6.6 Retry guidance — summary
docs.text: Operation Idempotent? Retry on 500/5xx? generate-payment-token Yes per (merchant, order_id) while a prior token is still valid Yes — same order_id returns the same token validate-payment Yes (read) Yes fetch-payment-status Yes (read) Yes fetch-payment-token-data Yes (read) Yes payment/direct Yes — the token's state machine prevents double-charge Yes, but verify with fetch-payment-token-data first cash-back Yes per reference_id Yes — same reference_id returns the same result transactions (list) Yes (read) Yes

docs.title: 7.1 Sandbox
docs.text:
Base URL https://dev.zicharge.com Purpose Functional integration testing, no real funds movement Lifetime Persistent; data is retained but not warranted Throughput Generous, but not load-tested — do not stress-test sandbox Test wallets Provisioned on request from integrations support

Sandbox shares the same wire contract as production. The only difference is that all settlement is simulated.

docs.title: 7.2 Test plan checklist
docs.text: Run this list against sandbox before requesting production credentials.

docs.title: Functional
docs.text:
• Mint a token with the canonical JSON request and the form-urlencoded request — verify both succeed.
  • Customer pays on the hosted page — verify the IPN arrives and fetch-payment-status returns paid: true.
  • Customer cancels on the hosted page — verify the cancel IPN arrives with status=Cancelled.
  • Let a token expire (15 min) — verify no IPN fires while it is unopened, and that opening it after expiry triggers a system cancel + cancel IPN.
  • Direct payment with a valid token — verify success envelope and IPN.
  • Direct payment with an already-used token — verify code 409.
  • Direct payment with an expired token — verify code 410.
  • fetch-payment-status for an unpaid order — verify paid: false and code 404 in the body.
  • fetch-payment-status for a paid order — verify paid: true and code 200.
  • validate-payment for a paid order — verify the full detail payload.
  • Cash-back to a known wallet — verify success.
  • Cash-back retry with the same reference_id — verify the same result is returned (idempotency).
  • List transactions, page through more than one page.

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Wrong store_password — code 401, generic message.
  • Malformed merchant_mobile_no — code 422.
  • bill_amount < 250 — code 422.
  • Missing required field — code 422, message names the field.
  • Direct-payment with a bad customer password — code 422, unified message.

docs.title: IPN listener
docs.text:
• Listener returns 2xx only after persistence.
  • Listener handles repeated delivery of the same X-ZiCharge-Request-Id and does not double-process.
  • If signatures are enabled: signature mismatch is rejected; valid signature is accepted.
  • Timestamp drift > 5 minutes is rejected.
  • Listener survives a brief outage and accepts the eventual retry.

docs.title: Resilience
docs.text:
• Inject a 500 from your listener — verify the gateway retries within the documented window.
  • After listener recovery, verify the retry succeeds and no duplicate is stored on your side.

docs.title: Reconciliation
docs.text: • Disable your IPN listener entirely, complete a payment, then run fetch-payment-status and verify you can recover the outcome.

docs.title: 7.3 Go-live checklist
docs.text:
When the sandbox plan is green:

  • Production merchant_mobile_no and store_password provisioned and stored in your secrets manager — never in source control.
  • Production ipn_url configured. HTTPS, public, your own ingress.
  • (Recommended) IPN signature verification enabled; ipn_secret stored in secrets manager.
  • (Recommended) Source IP allow-list configured for outbound server-to-server calls.
  • Your egress IPs registered with integrations support if you intend to allow-list at our edge.
  • Reconciliation cron configured for orders that pass a timeout without an IPN.
  • Logging: you record X-ZiCharge-Request-Id for every gateway interaction (outbound and IPN).
  • Alerting: at minimum, a non-2xx rate alert on your IPN listener and a "no IPN within N minutes after token mint" alert on your order pipeline.

docs.title: 7.4 Operational expectations
docs.text:
• Uptime target: 99.9% monthly on the production gateway.
  • Maintenance windows: announced in advance through the integrations channel.
  • Deploys: immutable, rolling — no downtime in the steady state. A small number of in-flight requests may receive a 5xx during instance shift; retries on the idempotent endpoints recover transparently.
  • P99 latency targets are documented in 03-payment-flow.md §3.7.

docs.title: 7.5 Credential rotation
docs.text:
• store_password — rotate from the merchant dashboard. The gateway supports a brief overlap window during rotation so you can deploy the new value with zero downtime.
  • ipn_secret — same rotation model: two active secrets supported during rotation.
  • Customer credentials — never handled by you in any form; customers rotate their own from the ZiCharge app.

docs.title: 7.6 Support
docs.text:
• Integration questions: integrations@zicharge.com (placeholder; replace with your actual channel).
  • Production incidents: the support escalation path documented in your merchant agreement.
  • Always include: X-ZiCharge-Request-Id, order_id, approximate timestamp, environment (sandbox / production).

docs.title: 8. Migration Notes — Previous Gateway vs. Current Java Engine
docs.text:
TL;DR for existing merchants: Your current integration keeps working. Endpoint URLs, request shapes, response shapes, and the IPN payload are preserved. There are seven small behavioural differences listed below — none are blockers if you follow the recommendations. New, optional capabilities (signed IPN, QR token endpoint, idempotency on cash-back) are additive.

The current gateway is a complete re-engineering of the merchant payment surface on a Java 21 / Spring Boot 3.2 backend. The re-engineering was scoped explicitly to preserve the journey your merchants already know while strengthening validation, idempotency, race-safety, and observability under the hood.

This chapter is the comparison map. Read it once during your upgrade audit, then keep it for reference.

docs.title: 8.1 What is unchanged (and warranted to stay so)
docs.text:
These items are part of the wire contract and are guaranteed within this major version.

Surface Status Endpoint paths — POST /merchant/generate-payment-token, POST /merchant/payment/validation, POST /merchant/payment/direct, the hosted GET /merchant/payment?token=... ✅ Same Equivalent canonical paths under /api/v3/merchant/... are also available (recommended for new code) ✅ Same behaviour Request field names — merchant_mobile_no, store_password, order_id, bill_amount, success_url, cancel_url, fail_url, customer_mobile_no, payment_token, mobile_no, password ✅ Same Credential model — merchant_mobile_no + store_password server-to-server, hosted page for customer credentials ✅ Same Hosted-page URL — https://<host>/merchant/payment?token=<token> ✅ Same Token validity window — 15 minutes from mint ✅ Same Response envelope — { messages, data, code }, HTTP 200 for all outcomes ✅ Same Legacy success envelope for generate-payment-token — { messages, token, code } ✅ Same Legacy success envelope for payment/direct — { messages, transactionInfo, code } ✅ Same IPN method, content-type, and field set — POST application/x-www-form-urlencoded with transaction_id, order_id, bill_amount, customer_account_no, status, received_at, ipn=1 ✅ Same IPN status values — Success, Cancelled ✅ Same Both JSON and form-urlencoded request bodies accepted on server-to-server endpoints ✅ Same

If your existing integration only uses the items above, it requires no changes.

docs.title: 8.2 Behavioural differences you should know about
docs.text: Each item below lists: the difference, the rationale, the impact on a live integration, and the recommended action.

docs.title: Δ-1 — Iraqi mobile-number regex is now enforced on payment endpoints
docs.text:
What changed merchant_mobile_no, customer_mobile_no (when present), and customer mobile_no on direct payment are validated against ^\+964(0)?[0-9]{9,10}$. Why The previous gateway also enforced this regex on the same endpoints. The Java engine was briefly more lax during the rebuild; this restores parity. Impact on you If your client was submitting numbers without the +964 prefix, they were failing downstream anyway. They now fail at validation with a clear 422. Action Send all mobiles in +964XXXXXXXXXX format. Validation message: "The merchant mobile no format is invalid." / "The customer mobile no format is invalid.". Blocker? ❌ No. Same behaviour as the previous gateway.

Note (intentional carve-out): Cash-back (POST /merchant/cash-back) does not enforce this regex on receiver_mobile_no, matching the previous gateway. The receiver is validated by existence in the user table.

docs.title: Δ-2 — Minimum bill_amount enforced at token generation
docs.text: What changed bill_amount < 250 is rejected at token mint with code 422, instead of being accepted and failing later at payment time. Why Fail-fast surfaces the error to your client at the moment your server has the full request context, not in the middle of a customer-facing checkout. Impact on you If you previously minted sub-minimum tokens for testing, you must now use ≥ 250. Production traffic that already succeeded at payment time is unaffected. Action Ensure your minimum order value is ≥ 250 IQD before calling token mint. Blocker? ❌ No. The previous behaviour was a bug; the engineered behaviour is the documented limit.

docs.title: Δ-3 — Direct-payment error message is unified
docs.text: What changed When the customer's credentials are invalid for any reason (unknown user, wrong password, blocked account), the message returned is the single string "Mobile No. & Password combination do not match.". The previous gateway also used this exact wording on the unified failure path. Why Prevents account enumeration. Impact on you If your client was string-matching distinct messages (e.g., "User blocked"), the unified message replaces them. Action Match on the unified string, or — better — match on the body code field. Blocker? ❌ No. The previous gateway already used the same string on the joined credential check.

docs.title: Δ-4 — fetch-payment-status does the amount match at the database
docs.text: What changed The match is now WHERE merchant_id = ? AND order_id = ? AND amount = ? at the SQL level. The previous gateway behaved the same; an intermediate Java version compared amounts in memory after a broader fetch. The current engine restores the legacy behaviour. Why Deterministic, indexed lookup. Eliminates a class of decimal-precision edge cases. Impact on you If you were sending a wrong amount, you now consistently receive "Awaiting payment...". There is no scenario where the previous gateway succeeded and the current one fails for the same (order, amount). Action None for correct integrations. Blocker? ❌ No. Parity with the previous gateway.

docs.title: Δ-5 — validate-payment returns code 422 on "order not found"
docs.text: What changed Previously some Java builds returned code 404 for "order not found"; the current engine returns code 422 with the message "Sorry! Invalid Order ID.", matching the previous gateway's body-code shape. Why Restores legacy body-code parity. Impact on you A client that switched on code == 404 for "order not found" must now switch on code == 422 (with the messages[] text identifying the case). Action Switch on code for credential errors (422 + "Sorry! Invalid Store Credentials.") and for missing-order (422 + "Sorry! Invalid Order ID."). Blocker? ⚠️ Audit your client. Most legacy clients already expect 422 because that is what the previous gateway returned. If your client coded against an interim Java version, update the branch.

docs.title: Δ-6 — Per-field enumeration probes removed
docs.text: What changed The previous gateway exposed a sequence of Laravel-style validator probes — for example, a field-level message saying the merchant mobile does not exist before the credential check. The current engine returns a generic "Sorry! Invalid Store Credentials." instead. Why The earlier behaviour leaked whether a given mobile number or store password existed anywhere in the system, which is an enumeration vector. Impact on you A client that string-matched the field-specific message ("The merchant mobile no does not exist.") will not see it. Action Match on the unified credential message. If your business workflow requires the distinct case (very unlikely), contact integrations support — a feature-flagged legacy mode exists for the transition. Blocker? ⚠️ Audit your client. This is a deliberate security improvement. If you find a hard dependency on the field-specific message, raise it before go-live.

docs.title: Δ-7 — Activity log row written on successful direct payment
docs.text: What changed After a successful direct payment, the engine writes an audit row to activity_logs (mirroring the legacy ActivityDone event). Why Compliance and audit completeness — closes a gap that existed in early Java builds. Impact on you None at the API level. The row is internal. Your client will not observe this. Action None. Blocker? ❌ No. Internal only.

docs.title: 8.3 New capabilities (opt-in, additive)
docs.text:
These are not present on the previous gateway. They are available immediately on the current engine. None of them break existing integrations.

Capability What it gives you How to adopt Canonical /api/v3/merchant/... paths Versioned, explicit prefix Use the new paths in new client code; old paths still work POST /merchant/generate-qr-token Same token + a server-generated QR URL + expires_in_minutes Replace generate-payment-token for in-store / kiosk / printed-media flows GET /merchant/fetch-payment-status & GET /merchant/fetch-payment-token-data Idempotent GET variants of the POST endpoints Use for retry-friendly polling and CORS-enabled browser inspection Signed IPN (X-ZiCharge-Signature + X-ZiCharge-Timestamp + X-ZiCharge-Request-Id) HMAC-SHA256 authenticity proof on every IPN; constant-time verification on your side Enable on the merchant dashboard; configure ipn_secret IPN retry with cooldown gate At-least-once delivery with bounded duplicate risk; up to 8 retries per terminal event Make your listener idempotent on X-ZiCharge-Request-Id Cash-back idempotency on reference_id Replay-safe cash-back / refunds Always supply a stable reference_id SSRF-hardened IPN dispatch Internal-range URLs are refused None — keep your ipn_url public-resolvable HTTPS Stronger token state machine Pessimistic lock + conditional UPDATE eliminates the historical "race between cancel and pay" class of bugs None — automatic First-page hosted UX polish Localized (en/ar/ku), responsive, iframe-embeddable, JS countdown, in-page cancel None — automatic Structured observability Every interaction tagged with X-ZiCharge-Request-Id; correlatable end-to-end Log the ID on your side, include in support requests

docs.title: 8.4 Blocker check — explicit verdict
docs.text:
The audit team compared the current Java implementation, endpoint by endpoint, against the documented legacy behaviour and the previous gateway's PHP source. Findings:

Concern Verdict Endpoint paths drift ✅ None. All legacy paths preserved; canonical /api/v3/merchant/... is additive. Request shapes drift ✅ None. Same field names, same types, same required/optional split. Response envelope drift ✅ None. { messages, data, code } for v3 endpoints; legacy envelopes preserved for generate-payment-token and payment/direct. HTTP status drift ✅ None. HTTP 200 across the board, outcome in body code. IPN contract drift ✅ None. Form-encoded POST, same fields, same values. New optional headers (signature, timestamp, request-id) are additive. Token lifetime drift ✅ None. 15-minute validity preserved. Validation drift ⚠️ Two intentional changes — Δ-1 (regex on payment endpoints — matches previous gateway), Δ-2 (min bill_amount at mint — matches the documented limit). Neither is a regression. Body-code drift ⚠️ One intentional change — Δ-5 (validate-payment returns 422 for missing order, matching the previous gateway). Only an interim Java version returned 404. Message wording drift ⚠️ One intentional change — Δ-6 (per-field enumeration messages removed; unified credential message used). Deliberate security improvement. Side-effect drift ✅ One additive change — Δ-7 (audit-log row written on direct-payment success). Not externally observable.

Verdict: no blockers for live merchants. All differences are either parity restorations against the previous gateway or deliberate, documented security improvements. The two items worth client-side auditing are Δ-5 (body code 422 on missing-order) and Δ-6 (unified credential message). Both can be handled by switching client logic from string-match to code-match — the recommended path even on the previous gateway.

docs.title: 8.5 Migration plan template
docs.text:
A safe path for an existing merchant to upgrade:

  1. Day 0 — point a sandbox client at https://dev.zicharge.com using the current engine. Run the test plan.
  2. Day 0–7 — audit your client for: hard-coded HTTP status checks (none should exist; if any do, switch to code), and for the two intentional message changes in Δ-5 and Δ-6.
  3. Day 7 — enable the signed-IPN feature on your sandbox merchant config; update your listener to verify signatures.
  4. Day 14 — sign off on the sandbox plan. Schedule production switch.
  5. Day 15 — flip production traffic. No DNS or endpoint change required — production already runs on the current engine. The "switch" is your own client deploy if any changes were needed.
  6. Day 15–30 — monitor IPN delivery success rate and reconciliation gaps using X-ZiCharge-Request-Id correlation.
